summary: We have a large market in Plant City, which is supposed to be the largest market in the world. When we take our strawberries there, we just drive right up and get paid immediately. I spent a few years in Plant City before settling here in Hillsborough County in 1942. The nearest doctor to our place is also located in Plant City, so it’s an important town for us. Ed often drives our old 1925 Ford Model T to Plant City to haul berries to market.

I was born in Madison, Florida, on January 15, 1876, and my wife was also born there, though she doesn’t know her exact birth date. All of her people were born around Madison, and her parents were old slaves married in that area. Madison is where our roots are, and it shaped much of our early life before we moved to Live Oak and later to Hillsborough County. It’s a place that holds a lot of family history for us.

I lived around Live Oak, Florida, for nearly eighteen years and was educated there up to the 11th grade. It was an important place for my schooling before I moved on to other parts of Florida. My education in Live Oak laid the foundation for my later studies, including some time at Florida Agricultural and Mechanical College. Live Oak was where I grew up and learned the value of education.

I came to Hillsborough County in 1942 and settled on this place that was once wilderness. When I arrived, the road was just a trail, and I cleared every spot on the land myself. It’s been a hard but rewarding life farming here, growing strawberries, beans, and many other crops. This county has become our home, and we’ve built a community here, including a Missionary Baptist Church where we worship twice a month.

I remember when the Florida Central Peninsular Railroad, or the C P as we called it, ran through here. It later became known as the Plant System and now is called the ACL. The old timers still refer to it as the Plant System. It was part of the early days when few Negro families lived out here, and it helped connect us to other places.

entity: New York State National Youth Administration
summary: I was one of thirteen girls chosen by the New York State National Youth Administration to attend a four-month course in Home Economics at Florida Agricultural and Mechanical College. The NYA paid all our expenses, and I enjoyed the work so much that I plan to return in the fall to complete the course. Someday, I hope to teach Home Economics, thanks to the opportunity the NYA gave me.

I belong to the Missionary Baptist Church at Antioch, where we have services twice a month. Our pastor comes out from Tampa, and we look forward to his visits because the services are so good. When I first settled here, there was no church, but as more families arrived, we started a small church. Religion is very important to me, and I believe it is the greatest power on earth alongside education.

My two brothers, James and Henry Gray, live in Tampa, Florida. Our pastor for the Missionary Baptist Church comes from Tampa to hold services twice a month. Tampa is a nearby city that connects us to family and religious life. Though I prefer the freedom of farming here, Tampa is an important place for us socially and spiritually.

I keep an old 1925 Ford Model T, which I use to haul my berries to market. It’s a reliable vehicle for traveling the dirt roads and getting my produce to town quickly. Even though it’s an older car, it’s essential for my farming business and helps me manage my crops and sales. I’m proud of it because it makes the work easier and connects me to the market in Plant City.
